result:
I’d like to write about a quiet, unassuming little thing: the act of listening. Not the kind of listening we do in conversation, waiting for our turn to speak, nor the distracted half-listening we offer to a podcast while scrolling. I mean the deep, porous, and vulnerable act of receiving the world without the immediate need to label, curate, or respond to it. It feels like a forgotten art, a radical act of stillness in a world built on broadcast.

It begins, I think, with turning down the internal noise. My own mind is a busy committee, often shouting over the witness on the stand. To truly listen, I must first hush that committee. I have to step outside the fortress of my own opinions, my running to-do list, my anxieties about what I’ll say next, and simply become a receptacle. It’s surprisingly difficult. It feels like idling, like waste. Our productivity-obsessed culture tells us that any moment not spent generating output is a moment lost. But listening is not passive; it’s an intense, active reception. It is work of a different kind—the work of the soul, perhaps, or the heart.

So, I practice. I sit on my back steps in the late afternoon, a time when the light is soft and apologetic. I close my eyes, and I try not to *do* anything. I just let the sounds arrive.

First, the obvious orchestra: the whoosh of a distant car, the mechanical hum of a neighbour’s air conditioner. These are the brass section, loud and declarative. But if I hold still, if I let my attention soften like a unfocusing lens, the subtler instruments emerge. A single leaf, crisp and dry, scratching its way along the pavement, pushed by an almost imperceptible breeze. It sounds like a secret being whispered. There’s the chatter of sparrows in the hedgerow—not a generic bird noise, but a specific conversation of chirps, trills, and warning ticks. I can’t understand it, but I can appreciate its layered complexity. A dog barks three streets over, and I can almost map the neighbourhood in my mind by the chain reaction of barks that follow, a ripple of canine telegraphy. Somewhere, a screen door slams with a tired, metallic sigh.

This symphony isn’t always bucolic. The growl of a low-flying plane stitches a line of industrial noise across the sky. A siren wails in the city’s core, a sound that never fails to hook a little barb of anxiety in my chest. But the practice isn’t about enjoying the sounds; it’s about hearing them. Really hearing them. Letting the siren be a wave of pitch and urgency, not just a symbol of tragedy. Letting the plane’s roar be a physical vibration in the air. It’s about receiving the world as it is, not as I interpret it to be.

This kind of listening changes your relationship with everything. You start to listen to people differently. Beyond their words, you hear the textures in their voice—the gravel of exhaustion, the slight tremor of excitement, the spaces between sentences where the real feeling pools. You hear the unspoken plea in a friend’s offhand complaint, the buried joy in their simple anecdote. It becomes less about the story they’re telling and more about the human being who is inhabiting this moment, sharing this fragile vibration of sound. It is the ultimate act of respect: I will hold this piece of you in the space of my attention. I will not colonize it with my own story just yet.

You listen to music differently, too. A song you’ve heard a hundred times suddenly reveals a buried harmony, the breath of the singer before a line, the faint tap of a drumstick on a rim. It becomes three-dimensional. You’re not just consuming a product; you’re exploring a landscape someone built out of silence and vibration.

Most profoundly, you begin—in flashes—to listen to yourself. To the quiet, insistent voice beneath the clamour of worry and ambition. The one that says, “I am tired,” not as a complaint, but as a fact. The one that whispers, “This matters,” or “Let this go.” We spend so much time arguing with ourselves, coaching, berating, analysing. But what if we just listened? Without judgment, without an agenda? It’s terrifying. That inner voice can be inconvenient. It might tell you to quit something you’ve invested years in, or to embrace something that seems frivolous. But it is the sound of your own truth, and it speaks in a register so quiet it can only be heard in the cathedral silence of deep listening.

I think we are starving for this. We are drowning in noise—curated playlists, 24-hour news, the endless, glittering chatter of social media. It’s all transmission. We are all little radio towers, blasting out our signal, desperate for someone to tune in. But the dial is stuck on ‘Broadcast.’ We have forgotten how to turn it to ‘Receive.’

When you practice listening, the world ceases to be a backdrop to your own drama. It becomes the main event. The dripping tap becomes a percussion solo. The wind in the wires becomes a mournful, humming choir. The laughter from an open window becomes a sudden, beautiful gift. You realize that every moment is saturated with this unseen, unheard music. The universe is not silent. It is a roaring, whispering, cracking, singing, sighing place. We’ve just forgotten how to hear it.

It’s in this state of deep listening that I feel most connected and, paradoxically, most at peace in my insignificance. I am not the composer of this grand, chaotic symphony; I am an audience member in the very last row. But I am present for it. I am taking it in. And in that act of humble reception, I am woven into the fabric of it all. The sound of the world enters me, vibrates in my bones, and for a moment, the boundary between ‘in here’ and ‘out there’ dissolves. I am not just hearing the wind. I am a space where the wind is heard.

It costs nothing. It requires no special equipment. It asks only for your attention, that most precious and squandered resource. So, I will keep practicing. I will keep trying to hush my inner committee. I will keep sitting on the steps as the light fades, a student in the world’s infinite, whispering classroom, learning to hear again.
